title: Color Mixing
primer: Color in a shader is just a vec3 or vec4 — three or four floats you can manipulate with ordinary arithmetic. The three workhorses are mix(a, b, t) (linear interpolation), clamp(x, lo, hi) (keep values in range), and step/smoothstep (binary or smooth thresholds). For richer palettes, converting from HSV (hue, saturation, value) lets you cycle through the full spectrum with a single floating-point parameter.

challenges.title: Sunset gradient
challenges.description: Create a vertical gradient that blends from deep blue at the bottom to warm orange at the top. Use mix() with uv.y as the blend factor.
hints: `mix(a, b, t)` returns `a * (1.0 - t) + b * t`. When t = 0.0 you get `a`; when t = 1.0 you get `b`.
Pass `uv.y` as the blend factor `t`. Remember that in OpenGL, uv.y = 0 is at the bottom and uv.y = 1 is at the top.
challenges.unlocks_theory: `mix(a, b, t)` is GLSL's linear interpolation (lerp) function. It works component-wise on any vector type — `mix(vec3, vec3, float)` interpolates all three channels simultaneously. The blend factor `t` can itself be a gradient, a noise value, or any other signal, making `mix` the universal connector between color values.

challenges.title: Clamped tinted circle
challenges.description: Draw a red circle (radius 0.3, center 0.5) that brightens toward the center using a radial gradient. Use clamp() to ensure the brightness stays in [0, 1] before assigning it as the red channel.
hints: A simple radial brightness is `1.0 - d * someScale`. Using a scale > 2.0 means the value goes negative before the edge — `clamp` keeps it from going below 0.0.
Try `clamp(1.0 - d * 2.5, 0.0, 1.0)`. Multiplying by the circle `mask` afterwards ensures the glow is cut off at the shape boundary.
challenges.unlocks_theory: `clamp(x, lo, hi)` is essential for keeping computed values within valid color ranges. Intermediate calculations often produce values outside [0, 1] — especially when combining multiple signals — and failing to clamp results in unwanted wrap-around or GPU-vendor-specific undefined behavior. Always clamp before writing to `fragColor`.

challenges.title: HSV color wheel
challenges.description: Implement an HSV-to-RGB conversion and use the angle around the canvas center as the hue. The result is a full color wheel: hue varies with angle, saturation increases with distance from center, value is constant at 1.0.
hints: `atan(y, x)` returns the angle in radians from -π to π. Divide by `2π` to normalize to [-0.5, 0.5], then add 0.5 to shift to [0, 1]. This is your hue.
Saturation increases with distance: `clamp(length(p) * 2.0, 0.0, 1.0)` gives 0 at the center (white) and 1 at the edges (full color).
challenges.unlocks_theory: HSV separates color into perceptually meaningful axes: hue (which color), saturation (how vivid), and value (how bright). This makes it far easier to generate pleasing palettes programmatically than working in RGB. Animating the hue with `u_time` produces smooth, never-clashing color cycles — a staple technique in demo scene shaders.

challenges.title: Animated color palette
challenges.description: Use the cosine palette formula to generate a smoothly animated, infinitely varied color from UV position and time. The formula is: color = a + b * cos(2π * (c * t + d)) where a, b, c, d are vec3 palette coefficients.
hints: The formula is `a + b * cos(2π * (c * t + d))`. In GLSL use `6.28318` (2π) as the constant. The `cos` and `*` operations work component-wise on `vec3`.
With `a = vec3(0.5)` and `b = vec3(0.5)`, the cosine oscillates between 0 and 1 per channel — producing colors that always stay in the valid range without any `clamp`.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The cosine palette is a compact, powerful technique by Inigo Quilez. Because cosine is bounded to [-1, 1], choosing a=0.5 and b=0.5 guarantees all RGB channels stay in [0, 1]. The four coefficient vectors give you precise control over brightness, contrast, frequency, and phase offset per channel — effectively a parameterized Fourier approximation of any smooth color ramp.
